DEFENDANTS NIESAR & VESTAL LLP, GERALD NIESAR, AND OSCAR ESCOBAR'S SECOND MOTION TO SEAL LODGED CONTAINING CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION AND TRADE SECRETS IS GRANTED. NO OPPOSITION FILED, AND THE MOTION COMPLIES WITH CALIFORNIA RULES OF COURT, RULES 2.550 AND 2.551. THE COURT FINDS THAT THERE EXISTS AN OVERRIDING INTEREST THAT OVERCOMES THE RIGHT OF PUBLIC ACCESS TO THE PORTIONS OF THE RECORDS TO BE SEALED; THE OVERRIDING INTEREST SUPPORTS SEALING THOSE RECORDS; A SUBSTANTIAL PROBABILITY EXISTS THAT THE OVERRIDING INTEREST WILL BE PREJUDICED IF THE RECORD IS NOT SEALED; THE PROPOSED SEALING IS NARROWLY TAILORED; AND NO LESS RESTRICTIVE MEANS EXIST TO ACHIEVE THE OVERRIDING INTEREST. ORDER IS SIGNED. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN, CLERK: S. LE, NOT REPORTED. =(302/EPS)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File Second Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Aug-06-2019 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Marjan Hajimirzaee, Esq. of Parker Law Group, Tel: 916.996.0400, for plaintiff and moving party, on Court Call;
Geoffrey T. MacBride, Esq. of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney, Tel: 415.788.1900, for defendant and opposing party.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of arguments, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on PLAINTIFFS BUENOFRIO INC. AND IGNACIO GUTIERREZ's Motion For Leave To File Second Amended Complaint as follows:

Plaintiffs Buenofrio, Inc. and Ignacio Gutierrez's motion for leave to file second amended complaint is denied. Contrary to Plaintiffs' assertion that the proposed amendments are based on facts they only recently learned in discovery, Defendants' showing establishes that Plaintiffs have been aware of many if not most of those facts since 2013 and 2014, and indeed that Plaintiffs previously attested to many of them in verified discovery responses more than one year ago. Further, the case was originally filed on March 29, 2017, and trial is now set for September 16, 2019, after having been previously continued several times. Allowing Plaintiffs at this late date to amend to add new claims and allegations would prejudice Defendants by requiring them to reopen discovery, potentially including re-deposing witnesses, imposing additional burden and expense, and delaying the trial yet again. (See Magpali v. Farmers Group, Inc. (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 471, 486-488 [no abuse of discretion in denial of request for leave to amend where plaintiff's amendment was proposed on the eve of trial, nearly two years after the complaint was originally filed, plaintiff failed to provide explanation for leaving claims out of original complaint or bringing the request to amend so late, and prejudice to defendant was clearly shown]; see also Fair v. Bakhtiari (2011) 195 Cal.App.4th 1135, 1147 ["In spite of this policy of liberality, a court may deny a good amendment in proper form where there is unwarranted delay in presenting it"].)

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Against Plaintiff Ignacio Gutierrez
Off Calendar Aug-05-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Geoffrey T. MacBride, Esq. of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney, Tel: 415.788.1900, appearing for defendants and moving party;
Port Parker, Esq. of Parker Law Group, Tel: 916.996.0400, appearing for plaintiff and opposing party.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of arguments, the court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANTS NIESAR & VESTAL, LLP, GERALD NIESAR, and OSCAR ESCOBAR, RENEWED MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT AGAINST PLAINTIFF IGNACIO GUTIERREZ as follows:

Defendants Niesar & Vestal LLP, Gerald Niesar, and Oscar Escobar's motion for summary judgment on Ignacio Gutierrez's claims is denied.

Defendants fail to show that Mr. Gutierrez's malpractice claim is time-barred per Code of Civil Procedure, Sec. 340.6. Defendants do not establish as a matter of law that Mr. Gutierrez incurred "actual injury" on February 24, 2014, when the voting trust was created. Rather, the record shows that he sustained actual injury on April 1, 2016, when the Asset Purchase Agreement was executed and he allegedly received an inadequate purchase price. (Defendants' UMF 68, 73.) (See, e.g., Jordache Enterprises, Inc. v. Brobeck, Phleger & Harrison (1998) 18 Cal.4th 739, 765 [for purposes of Sec. 340.6, "actual injury occurs when the plaintiff sustains any loss or injury legally cognizable as damages in a legal malpractice action based on the acts or omissions that the plaintiff alleged"]; Callahan v. Gibson, Dunn & Crutcher LLP (2011) 194 Cal.App.4th 557, 573-575 [defective provisions in limited partnership agreement caused only contingent harm until general partner became incapacitated].) Plaintiff timely filed this action within one year of that date.

The court concludes that there is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether Mr. Gutierrez could have obtained a better result but for defendants' malpractice as explained in the companion motion. Further, Defendants concede that the Monogram deal would have provided additional funds for Mr. Gutierrez. (Reply at 3, 6.)

Defendants' arguments regarding disgorgement and unfair competition standing fail as they do not completely dispose of all of Mr. Gutierrez's claims and defendants do not seek summary adjudication.

Lastly, the court finds that Mr. Escobar is not entitled to summary judgment. There is a dispute of fact regarding whether the parties intended to waive claims as to him. The body of the letter agreement indicates that Mr. Gutierrez was waiving claims as to PLM and its affiliates only. He was not waiving claims against his attorneys. In fact, the letter agreement expressly preserves claims against "Niesar & Vestal, LLP." Mr. Escobar was a partner of Niesar & Vestal, LLP. (FACC Para. 3.) The waiver is reasonably susceptible to Mr. Gutierrez's interpretation that his claims against Mr. Escobar were preserved. (See Gutierrez Decl., Para. 42; Wolf v. Superior Court (2004) 114 Cal.App.4th 1343, 1351 [explaining that the trier of fact resolves meaning of ambiguous contract where the parole evidence is disputed].)

Prevailing party to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim the court's ruling and submit it to the court in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendant Niesar And Vestal Llp, Gerald Niesar, And Oscar Escobars Renewed Notice Of Motion And Motion For Summary Judgment Against Plaintiff Buenofrio, Inc.
Off Calendar Aug-05-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Geoffrey T. MacBride, Esq. of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney, Tel: 415.788.1900, appearing for defendants and moving party;
Port Parker, Esq. of Parker Law Group, Tel: 916.996.0400, appearing for plaintiff and opposing party.

RULING: The matter was argued. At the conclusion of arguments, the court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANTS NIESAR & VESTAL, LLP, GERALD NIESAR, and OSCAR ESCOBAR'S RENEWED MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT AGAINST PLAINTIFF BUENOFRIO, INC. as follows:

Defendants Niesar & Vestal LLP, Gerald Niesar, and Oscar Escobar's motion for summary judgment on Buenofrio Inc.'s claims is denied.

There is a triable issue of material fact regarding whether "but for the alleged malpractice, it is more likely than not that the plaintiff would have obtained a more favorable result." (Viner v. Sweet (2003) 30 Cal.4th 1232, 1244 (emphasis in original).) There is a dispute of fact whether the Monogram deal was better and whether defendants were negligent by not pursuing it. Under the Monogram proposal, Monogram was to pay over $16 million and Mr. Gutierrez would have received $4 million. (Plaintiffs' Evidence, Ex. YYYYYY.) Under the Canum Asset Purchase Agreement, Canum paid approximately $10 million and Mr. Gutierrez received $1.3 million. (Defendants' UMF 62.) The Vickery declaration indicates that PLM should have been sold for substantially more than the $10.1 million purchase price. (Vickery Decl. Para. 8.) There is also a dispute whether the Monogram deal could have been consummated. There is evidence that CVF assumed without an investigation that Monogram did not have adequate funding. (Blanco Depo., 213:1-214:17.)

(See word minutes for the court's complete ruling.)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To File First Amended Answers
Off Calendar Nov-06-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Geoffrey Macbride, Esq. 415-788-1900 of Murphy Pearson Bradley & Feeney for Defendant GERALD NIESAR, OSCAR ESCOBAR, NIESAR & VESTAL, LLP.
No other Appearance.

RULING - The matter is Uncontested. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT GERALD NIESAR, OSCAR ESCOBAR, NIESAR & VESTAL, LLP's Motion For Leave To File First Amended Answers.

Defendants Niesar & Vestal, LLP, Gerald Niesar, and Oscar Escobar's motion for leave to file first amended answers is GRANTED. Courts exercise liberality in permitting amendments at any stage of the proceeding and particularly use a liberal policy in allowing amendments to answers. (Hulsey v. Koehler (1990) 218 Cal.App.3d 1150, 1159 ("In particular, liberality should be displayed in allowing amendments to answers, for a defendant denied leave to amend is permanently deprived of a defense.").) While Defendants appear to have overlooked the fact that the waiver was in their possession prior to Plaintiffs' production of documents, Plaintiffs have not demonstrated that they will be prejudiced by allowing an amendment to the answer at this stage of litigation, while discovery is ongoing and trial is still five months away. Waxman v. Superior Court of Los Angeles County (1966) 246 Cal.App.2d 668 is distinguishable. In Waxman, the defendant sought to amend his answer 17 months after filing the original answer. (Id. at 671.) The defendant failed to explain why the affirmative defense was not included in the original answer, discovery had been completed, and the new defense would have required additional discovery that would have delayed trial. (Id.) By contrast, Defendants here brought this motion under a year after filing the original answer and they included an explanation that they overlooked the waiver and did not understand its import until receiving it in the context of Plaintiffs' responses. Plaintiffs have not indicated what new discovery would be required to respond to the waiver defense or how this amendment would prejudice them.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Suzanne R. Bolanos, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/SRB)
